coredata.dc:title: Chapter 7 Carbon Dots and Their Polymeric Nanocomposites
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2019 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: De, Bibekananda
coredata.dc:description: Currently, carbon dots (CDs) are emerging as one of the brightest and youngest carbon nanomaterials for intensive research in the domain of materials science. They have great potential to be used in a broad field of applications such as optoelectronic, energy, biomedical, sensors, catalysis, etc. owing to their excellent aqueous dispersibility, functionality, biocompatibility, and ease of preparation from different carbon-based resources. All these favorable attributes, along with large numbers of polar surface functional groups, make CDs one of the potential nanomaterials to fabricate polymer nanocomposites, which provide a dramatic improvement in performance as well as offers a new set of properties. These CD/polymer nanocomposites are widely used in optoelectronics, biomedical, catalysis, and energy applications. Therefore this chapter provides a decent and updated coverage on the fabrication, characterization, properties, and applications of CD/polymer nanocomposites. The chapter also presents a comprehensive account on synthesis, structural details, properties, and application, of different CDs.

chapter.ce:label: Chapter 7
chapter.ce:title: Carbon Dots and Their Polymeric Nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, IIT Kanpur, Kanpur, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, carbon dots (CDs) are emerging as one of the brightest and youngest carbon nanomaterials for intensive research in the domain of materials science. They have great potential to be used in a broad field of applications such as optoelectronic, energy, biomedical, sensors, catalysis, etc. owing to their excellent aqueous dispersibility, functionality, biocompatibility, and ease of preparation from different carbon-based resources. All these favorable attributes, along with large numbers of polar surface functional groups, make CDs one of the potential nanomaterials to fabricate polymer nanocomposites, which provide a dramatic improvement in performance as well as offers a new set of properties. These CD/polymer nanocomposites are widely used in optoelectronics, biomedical, catalysis, and energy applications. Therefore this chapter provides a decent and updated coverage on the fabrication, characterization, properties, and applications of CD/polymer nanocomposites. The chapter also presents a comprehensive account on synthesis, structural details, properties, and application, of different CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past two decades polymer nanocomposites of different carbon-based nanomaterials are gaining significant interest in both academic and industrial scenarios. A number of studies have been reported in the literature on different polymer nanocomposites of carbon-based nanomaterials from zero-dimensional fullerene, one-dimensional carbon nanotubes, and carbon nanofibers to two-dimensional graphene and graphene based materials (Moniruzzaman and Winey, 2006; Laiho et al., 2008; Al-Saleh and Sundararaj, 2009; Roy et al., 2010; Kim et al., 2010; Mittal et al., 2015). However, strong van der Waals forces affect the dispersion ability of these nanomaterials in the polymer matrices, and thus they have to be functionalized by strong acids or other oxidizing agents. Furthermore the optical properties, including transparency of the pristine polymers, is also often lost even below 1wt% loading with these carbon-based nanomaterials. In this vein, polymer/quantum dot nanocomposites have been widely utilized to achieve high transparency and high luminescence solid films for advanced optoelectronics (Park et al., 2004; Tomczak et al., 2009, 2013). However the well dispersion of semiconductor quantum dots in the polymer matrices is the key challenges to obtain highly transparent photoluminescent materials due to their organophobic surface. In addition the biocompatibility and toxicity of these nanomaterials can cause problems in many applications including biomedical.

Recently, carbon dots (also known as carbon quantum dots, C-dots, CDs, or CQDs) have emerged as a new class of attractive zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with size <10nm (Jia et al., 2012). Attribute of their highly fluorescence property, quantum dot size and the presence of a large number of oxygen functional groups, CDs have drawn increasing attention as a wonderful nanomaterial in polymer nanocomposites, in recent years. Therefore, they have exceptional advantages, such as high optical absorptivity, aqueous solubility, functionalizability, resistance to photobleaching, chemical stability, toxicity, and biocompatibility, compared to other carbon based materials and traditional semiconductor quantum dots (Li et al., 2012a,b; De and Karak, 2013). They gradually became exciting nanomaterials because of their benign and inexpensive nature plus ease of availability, which resulted in numerous possible applications in optoelectronic devices, catalysis, energy storage and harvesting, sensing, biological labeling, and biomedicines (Baker and Baker, 2010; Li et al., 2012a,b; De and Karak, 2013, 2017). The high dispersibility and compatibility due to the presence of large oxygen-containing groups on the surface, as well as nontoxicity and interesting optical properties of CDs mark them as one of the valuable nanomaterials for the fabrication of polymer nanocomposites. Currently, the polymer/CD nanocomposites are gaining significant attention in all aspects and applications of the material science domain, including optoelectronics, catalysis, biomedical, sensors, energy, etc. Therefore, this chapter provides a decent and updated coverage on the synthesis/fabrication, characterization, properties, and applications of CDs and their polymer nanocomposites.
CDs are among the youngest and brightest nanomaterials in the carbon family.

They were first discovered accidentally by Xu et al. (2004) during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs). They were noticed as an impurity while separating SWCNTs using gel electrophoresis from carbon soot produced by arc discharge. They isolated these water-soluble, highly fluorescent nanoparticles and defined them as fluorescent carbon nanoparticles. Later, Sun et al. (2006) reported strongly photoluminescent similar carbon nanoparticles, dispersed in solvent as well as extract in solid state, and they referred to them as carbon dots (CDs). They enhanced the fluorescence emission through surface passivation, which is very useful for optical labeling and bioimaging applications; subsequently, it was popularized as carbon or carbogenic or carbon quantum/nanodot in the literature (Baker and Baker, 2010; Li et al., 2012a,b; De and Karak, 2017). It shows fascinating physical, chemical, biological, electrical and optical properties according to its source, synthetic roots, size and surface chemistry, which make it an attractive material in the arenas of biomedical science, sensors, optoelectronics, catalysis, energy devices and so on (Baker and Baker, 2010; Li et al., 2012a,b,2015; Lim et al., 2015; De and Karak, 2017). Recently, polymeric nanocomposites of CD are widely utilized in solid-state optical films, heterogeneous catalysis, biomedical, sensor, etc. applications (De et al., 2013, 2014, 2015; Hazarika and Karak, 2016; Duarah et al., 2016; Saikia and Karak, 2018). The small size and large numbers of oxygen functional groups of CD provide strong physicochemical interactions, thereby homogeneous dispersion inside the polymer matrices is possible to achieve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Carbon Dots, Carbon Dot-Based Materials, and Their Modifications
ce:section.ce:para: Since the first discovery of CDs in 2004 through the arc discharge of soot (Xu et al., 2004), a variety of routes have been developed using numerous carbon precursors to obtain facile, cost-effective, size-controllable, and large-scale CDs as well as advanced functions with different compositions and structures for several applications. CDs can be prepared by both top-down and bottom-up approaches (De and Karak, 2017). Generally, in the top-down approach, CDs are obtained through breaking the large carbon clusters by chemical oxidation or by physical cleavage, whereas in the bottom-up approach a series of chemical reactions are utilized to obtain CDs from different carbon based precursors. A schematic representation is shown in Fig. 7.1 for the preparative techniques of CDs through top-down and bottom-up approaches.

Top-down approaches prepare CDs using macroscopic carbon structures (e.g., graphite, activated carbon, carbon soot, carbon nanotubes, etc.) through treatments such as arc-discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical and chemical oxidation, and ultrasonication (Lim et al., 2015; De and Karak, 2017; Wang et al., 2017b).

The first synthesis process used for the preparation of CDs was arc discharge (Xu et al., 2004). CDs were accidentally produced during the purification of SWCNTs obtained from carbon soot by the arc discharge process. Carbon soot was oxidized with nitric acid to improve its hydrophilicity and to obtain SWCNTs by the electrophoretic method. However, two new classes of nanomaterials were produced from the crude soot. One of them was the short, tubular carbon nanotubes, while the other one was an impurity, a mixture of unknown fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, which is later known as CDs.

Later a laser ablation method was used to obtain more morphology controlled and surface-passivated CDs. CDs are prepared by the laser ablation of a carbon source in the presence of water vapor with argon as the carrier gas, followed by surface passivation by attaching simple organic species. The carbon source is prepared by the hot pressing of a mixture of graphite powder and cement, followed by stepwise baking, curing, and finally annealing under an argon atmosphere. Different carbon-based materials such as graphite, nanocarbons, etc. are also used as carbon sources to prepare CDs by the laser ablation method. Sun et al. (2006) prepared CDs by the laser ablation of graphite under an argon flow in the presence of water vapor. Subsequently, Hu et al. (2011) synthesized size-controlled CDs by tuning the laser pulse width, which can have impacts on the nucleation and growth of CDs.

Electrochemical oxidation is one of the most common methods to prepare CDs due to the advantages like high purity, low cost, high yield, easy manipulation of size, and good reproducibility (Wang et al., 2017b). In this method, graphite and graphitic materials are used as the working electrode, along with platinum (Pt) as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode in different aqueous electrolytes to produce CDs (De and Karak, 2017). Zhou et al. (2007) have first demonstrated the electrochemical synthesis of CDs from MWCNTs. Size-controllable CDs with benign optical and electronic properties are also prepared through an alkali-assisted electrochemical method (Li et al., 2010b). Later, Li et al. (2011b) utilized a direct electrochemical synthesis for green luminescent CDs with a uniform size of 3–5nm. In this electrochemical oxidation process a graphene electrode is used in a phosphate buffer solution. The chemical oxidation process is also used to produce CDs, where different types of carbon soot obtained from burning candles, combustion chambers, etc. were oxidized by strong nitric acid or by other oxidizing agents (Liu et al., 2007; Tian et al., 2009). This is an effective and convenient approach for the large-scale production of CDs and in this instance requires no complicated devices.

Li et al. (2011a) utilized the ultrasonic method to synthesize CDs. Here, water-soluble CDs are synthesized from activated carbon by a one-step hydrogen peroxide-assisted ultrasonic treatment. In the ultrasonic method, ultrasound generates alternating low-pressure and high-pressure waves in the liquid medium, which subsequently form and collapse small vacuum bubbles. These cavitations cause high-speed interrupting liquid jets, deagglomeration, and strong hydrodynamic shear forces that can cut macroscopic carbon materials into nanoscale CDs (Wang et al., 2017b).

In these synthetic approaches the size of CDs are controlled and separated through posttreatment, such as gel electrophoresis, centrifugation, and dialysis. In maximum cases the different sizes of CDs are commonly separated by a high-speed centrifugation technique using a water-ethanol-chloroform solvent mixture. However, these top-down approaches have several disadvantages, like the necessity for a series of experiments, need for strong acids or electrolytes, and surface passivating agents for the improvement of solubility and fluorescence properties (De and Karak, 2017).

Different bottom-up approaches are introduced to address the drawbacks of top-down methods for the preparation of CDs. These approaches generally synthesize quantum-sized CDs from small organic molecules through polymerization reactions and are followed by carbonization using hydrothermal, pyrolysis, microwave-assisted pyrolysis, ultrasonic, and dehydration/direct heating techniques (De and Karak, 2017). Different organic molecules like glucose, sucrose, fructose, ascorbic acid, glycerol, chitosan, and ethylene diamine tetra-acetic acid are used as carbon precursors for the preparation of CDs.

The hydrothermal technique is the most facile, nontoxic, cost-effective one-pot method for the preparation of water-soluble CDs containing a large number of oxygenous groups. In this method the carbonization of small molecules takes place at an elevated temperature (150–200°C) and pressure in the presence of water using a sealed hydrothermal reactor (Yang et al., 2011,2012; Hsu and Chang, 2012). After further extraction and centrifugation, highly fluorescent, size-controlled and water-soluble CDs are obtained.

The synthesis of CDs using microwave irradiation of organic compounds is also a rapid and low-cost method (Zhu et al., 2009; Zhai et al., 2012). Microwave is a kind of electromagnetic wave with a large wavelength range from 1mm to 1m, which can provide intensive energy to break off the chemical bonds of a molecule. Therefore this method can meritoriously shorten the reaction time and provide simultaneous homogeneous heating, which leads to a uniform size distribution of CDs (Wang et al., 2012,2017b). Zhu et al. (2009) first prepared CDs with this method by using carbohydrates as a carbon source and PEG200 as both the solvent and coating agent. The reaction medium is irradiated using a 500W microwave power for a short time of 2–10min, where the color of the reaction medium gradually transforms to dark brown from a colorless solution. CDs are also obtained within 1min under microwave irradiation, using sucrose as the carbon source and diethylene glycol (DEG) as the reaction media (Liu et al., 2014). These DEG-stabilized CDs with an average diameter about 5nm are well-dispersed in water with a transparent appearance.

Pyrolysis under reflux conditions (Lai et al., 2012), ultrasonic (Park et al., 2014), direct heating or thermal decomposition (De and Karak, 2013; Ma et al., 2015) are also cost-effective and simple approaches for a large-scale synthesis of water-soluble CDs. The basic mechanism for the formation of CDs is the same in all of these bottom-up approaches (Sahu et al., 2012; De and Karak, 2013,2017). In the first step, different carbon precursors generally undergo hydrolysis, dehydration, and decomposition under suitable conditions and result in soluble compounds such as furfural aldehydes, ketones, and several organic acids like acetic, levulinic, formic, etc. Subsequently the aromatization and carbonization of these products take place through polymerization, condensation, and cycloaddition reactions. In the final step, CDs are formed from the aromatic clusters at a critical concentration of supersaturation point by a probable nuclear burst (De and Karak, 2013).

Recently, in order to produce large-scale CDs without using expensive carbon precursors, complex instrumental set-ups, or posttreatments, different green synthetic approaches were introduced. The current trend is to obtain high-quality fluorescent and biocompatible CDs, which is mainly focused on inexpensive bioprecursors as carbon sources. Sahu et al. (2012) produced highly photoluminescent CDs through a one-step hydrothermal method using orange juice as the starting precursor, as shown in Fig. 7.2A. The sizes of the CDs are obtained around 1.5–4.5nm after separation through high centrifugation force and are used in bioimaging due to their low toxicity and high photostability. We have also developed a simple synthetic procedure to prepare water-soluble fluorescent CDs with an average size of 3nm by the direct heating of banana juice at 150°C (De and Karak, 2013), as shown in Fig. 7.2B. In this method, narrow size distributions of CDs are also obtained through high centrifugation force. Subsequently, different green methods are developed utilizing different inexpensive and biocompatible precursors, such as ethanol, citrate, glucosamine, ascorbic acid, saccharides, candle soot, watermelon peels, pomelo peels, strawberry juice, sugar cane juice, chicken eggs, chitosan, organoge, Colocasia esculenta extract, glycerol, gelatin, many waste food items, grass, plant leaves, and so on (Sahu et al., 2012; De and Karak, 2013; Zheng et al., 2015; Lim et al., 2015; Fernando et al., 2015). Most of these biobased CDs displayed excellent performance in cell imaging and chemical sensing applications, though there is nothing unique about the CDs prepared with many of these selected precursors because the carbonization mechanism and the intermediate and final products are similar. The varieties of precursor doses suggest that CDs are not so picky with respect to the purity or exact composition of the underlying dot materials beyond the predominating carbon content (Fernando et al., 2015).

It is very important to control the morphology of CDs in order to obtain uniform properties for specific applications and mechanistic study. Many strategies have been demonstrated to prevent carbonaceous aggregation during carbonization and to obtain uniform and morphology controlled CDs during preparation or posttreatment. In most of the cases the as-synthesized CDs fragments are purified via post treatments such as filtration, dialysis, centrifugation, column chromatography, and gel-electrophoresis (Wang and Hu, 2014; Zheng et al., 2015). Liu et al. (2007) used poly(acrylamide) gel electrophoresis to separate CDs for obtaining monodispersed multicolor CDs, synthesized from candle soot. The excitation-dependent PL of the fractionated CDs is absent because of size uniformity. However, it is of great importance to control the morphology of CDs during the preparation. Discrete CDs with controlled morphology can be prepared via the confined pyrolysis of an organic precursor in nanoreactors, which consists of three steps: (a) absorption of the organic precursor into porous nanoreactors via capillary force, (b) pyrolysis of the organic precursor confined in the nanoreactors into carbonaceous matter, (c) and release of the as-synthesized CDs by removing the nanoreactors, as shown in Fig. 7.3 . The morphology of the produced CDs from this method is dictated by the texture parameters of the nanoreactors (Shen et al., 2012). Porous silica is the most widely used nanoreactor due to its versatility, tenability, and ease of texture formation, as well as its thermal stability and easy removal (Liu et al., 2009; Zong et al., 2011; Wang and Hu, 2014). Zong et al. (2011) synthesized hydrophilic CDs by pyrolysis at 300°C for 2h in air using mesoporous silica nanospheres as nanoreactors and citric acid precursors. After the removal of silica and followed by dialysis, CDs with a uniform size of 1.5–2.5nm are obtained, which exhibit good photostability, low toxicity, excellent luminescence, and up-conversion properties.

Thermally unstable polymers are also used to synthesize morphology controlled CDs by using them as block matter to avoid carbonaceous aggregation during thermal treatment (Zhu et al., 2013b; Wang and Hu, 2014). Here CDs are prepared with tunable and uniform sizes from single-chain polymeric nanoparticles, which are formed by a Bergman cyclization-intermediated chain collapse of linear polymers containing enediyne units. The sizes of the obtained CDs are tuned by changing the length of the polymeric chains or by grafting degrees of the enediyne in each chain.

Surface modification is a potential technique used to tune the properties of CDs for particular applications. Generally the surface of CDs has been modified through oxidation, functionalization, or passivation techniques. The presence of oxygen functional groups on CDs act as surface emissive traps resulting in high photoluminescence (PL) efficiency (Zheng et al., 2015; De and Karak, 2017). Therefore the fluorescent properties of the CDs can be tailored by controlling the degree of oxidation. It has been revealed that the reduction of oxygenated groups on CDs can enhance quantum yield, whereas the oxidation of CDs redshifts the emission (Zheng et al., 2015).

The oxygen functional groups of CDs also render them hydrophilic and provide convenient chemical handles for further functionalization. Therefore, surface functionalization of CDs with various molecules such as organic or inorganic molecules and polymers is an effective way to tailor the properties of CDs by controlling their surface states. Surface passivation through the covalent bonding of amine-containing agents is a common method to improve the fluorescence properties of CDs (Liao et al., 2013). Liao et al. (2013) synthesized CDs through the hydrothermal carbonization of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA·2Na) followed by functionalization with spiropyrans. The emission of the functionalized CDs centered at 510nm was switched off, while they were turned on at 650nm through energy transfer after UV light irradiation; the process is reversed by using visible light irradiation. Hola et al. (2014) reported alkyl chain-passivated CDs with controlled size and surface functionalization. The alkyl chains on the surface of CDs are converted to carboxylate groups through mild base NaOH hydrolysis, which induced the emission redshifted (from 402 to 440nm) due to COO− surface states. Polymer passivation of surface emissive traps of CDs is also used to improve the quantum yield. Poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) is the most commonly used polymer for the passivation of the surface of CDs (Shen et al., 2011), although other polymers like poly(ethylene imine) have also been reported (Liu et al., 2012).

Doping is another effective way to tune the properties of CDs by changing their electronic structures. Generally, heteroatoms like N-, S-, and P-doping have been used to tune or obtain new PL and other physicochemical properties of CDs. N-doping is the most studied way to enhance the emission of CDs by inducing an upward shift in the Fermi level and electrons in the conduction band (Ayala et al., 2010). It has been revealed that only the nitrogen bonding to the carbon can really enhance the PL emission of CDs, and the N-doped CDs show nitrogen content-dependent PL intensities with multicolor and two-photon up-conversion properties (Zhai et al., 2012; Xu et al., 2013). S- and N-codoped CDs have been synthesized through the carbonization and etching of hair fibers using sulfuric acid (Sun et al., 2013), where different binding configurations are found, such as a CS covalent bond of the thiophene-S and CSOx (x =2, 3, 4, sulfate or sulfonate) for S-doped, pyridinic N and pyrrolic N for N-doped, respectively. The resultant CDs exhibited high solubility, good luminescence stability, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility. The P- and N-codoped CDs have been prepared through the microwave irradiation of DMF in the presence of phosphoric acid (Prasad et al., 2013), which also exhibited excellent photoluminescent characteristics with high electrocatalytic activity. The obtained CDs also showed good tolerance to the methanol crossover effects in the oxygen reduction reaction in an alkaline medium.

The PL characteristic of CDs can be quenched efficiently either by electron acceptors or by electron donor molecules because photoexcited CDs can act as excellent electron donors as well as acceptors. This interesting phenomenon of CDs offers exciting opportunities in light energy conversion devices and in photocatalysis (De and Karak, 2017). Therefore CD metal-based nanohybrids have been extensively employed for several photochemical and electrochemical applications owing to their interesting photo-induced electron/charge transfer properties as well as up-conversion PL (De and Karak, 2017). These CD metal-based nanohybrids are mainly fabricated by physical mixing, chemical reduction, and hydrothermal/solvothermal methods (De and Karak, 2017). In the physical mixing technique, CD metal-based nanohybrids are fabricated through the mixing of a solution of CDs with a dispersion of metal nanoparticles by mechanical shearing followed by sonication (Li et al., 2010b,2012a,b; Tang et al., 2013; Mondal et al., 2014; Bhattacharya and Deb, 2015). Whereas in the chemical reduction technique, nanohybrids are prepared using CDs as the reducing agent as the peripheral oxygen containing groups of CDs (e.g., hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxylic acid and epoxy) that are capable of reducing metal ions by transferring electrons (Mitra et al., 2011; Choi et al., 2013; Dey et al., 2013; Shen et al., 2013; De et al., 2014; De and Karak, 2017), as shown in Fig. 7.4A. In this technique, CDs also act as stabilizing agents during the formation of metal-based nanoparticles due to the presence of functional groups on their surface. Therefore the growth of the metal nanoparticles starts on the surface of CDs to form the nanohybrids, as shown in Fig. 7.4B. However, currently the most widely used technique for the fabrication of CD metal-based nanohybrids is hydro/solvo-thermal, as it is a facile one-pot approach to achieving the controlled size and shape of the nanohybrids as well as establishing strong interactions between the metal nanoparticles and CDs. In this technique, nanohybrids are prepared through the mixing of the metal salts with either CDs or carbon precursors of CDs in an aqueous/organic/mixture of aqueous-organic medium before performing the required hydro/solvothermal treatment (Zhang et al., 2011,2013; Yu et al., 2014; Tang et al., 2014; De and Karak, 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and Characterization of Polymeric Nanocomposites of CD
ce:section.ce:para: Polymer nanocomposites are unique and interacting mixtures of polymer matrices with solid phase materials of at least one dimension in the nanometer size range. The uniform dispersion of the nanoscale particles produces an ultra-large interfacial area per unit volume between the nanoparticles and the polymer matrices, which improves the properties dramatically as well as provides a new set of desired properties to expand the application area of the virgin polymer. Therefore, to obtain a strong interaction between the nanomaterials and the polymer matrices, three techniques are generally used to fabricate polymer nanocomposites, such as solution, in situ, and melt mixing. However, due to the presence of a large number of oxygen-polar functional groups and quantum dot size, CDs and CD-based nanohybrids are easily integrated in polymer matrices with uniform dispersions and strong interfacial interactions through solution and in situ techniques (De et al., 2013, 2014, 2015; Hazarika and Karak, 2016; Duarah et al., 2016; Saikia and Karak, 2018). The uniform dispersion and strong interfacial interactions between CDs and polymer matrices after the formation of the nanocomposites are characterized and established through different spectroscopic, microscopic, and diffraction techniques.

In this technique a suitable solvent is used to disperse the CDs or CD-based nanohybrids into the polymer solution using high shearing and/or ultrasonic forces so that polymer chains can thoroughly interact with the CDs by breaking the self-interactions between them (De et al., 2013, 2014). Then the nanocomposite is formed by precipitation or by the evaporation of the solvents. We have fabricated CD nanocomposites by this technique with hyperbranched epoxy using a very small amount of ethanol-dispersed CDs (0.1%–1.0%) (De et al., 2013). The polar functional groups of CDs participate in the crosslinking reaction during the curing reaction with the poly(amido-amine) hardener, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 7.5A. Therefore the OH stretching frequency of the pristine polymer in FTIR is shifted from 3391 to 3290cm−1 after the formation of nanocomposites due to the strong interactions and chemical crosslinking of CDs with a hyperbranched epoxy and poly(amido-amine) hardener. The carbonyl peaks of CDs (1730cm−1) are also shifted to 1670cm−1 after the formation of nanocomposites because of the formation of amide linkages by the reaction of carboxylic acid and ester groups of CDs with the amino groups of the hardener.

However, as usual formation of polymer/CD nanocomposites by solution technique, suffers from some difficulties like use of large amount of solvents, choice, and removal of solvent, health hazards and safety associated with the solvents, etc. as well as the level of interactions between the polymers and CDs are also not so strong as compared to the in situ technique.

In this technique the oxygen functional groups of CDs are utilized as reactive components or monomers during the synthesis of the polymers (De et al., 2015; Hazarika and Karak, 2016). The properties of the nanocomposites achieved by this technique are superior compared to the solution technique. Our group fabricated in situ polymer nanocomposites of CDs with different types of polymers, like hyperbranched epoxy, polyester, polyurethane, etc. through the polymerization of CDs as the reactants with several monomers or prepolymers (De et al., 2015; Gogoi et al., 2015, 2017; Hazarika and Karak, 2016; Duarah et al., 2016; Ghosh et al., 2016). In our earlier study, 1wt% of CDs was used as a reactive monomer during the synthesis of pentaerythritol, bisphenol-A, and epichlorohydrin-based hyperbranched epoxy (De et al., 2015). The possible intermediate reactions involved during the synthesis and structure of the nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 7.5B. In this fabrication process, diglycidyl ether of bisphenol-A and epichlorohydrin were reacted with the reactive sodium salt of CDs and pentaerythritol to form a hyperbranched epoxy in situ nanocomposite. The diglycidyl ether of bisphenol-A and epichlorohydrin reacted with sodium salt of CDs through an anionic ring-opening polymerization mechanism. Therefore owing to this participation in different chemical reactions during the formation of a hyperbranched epoxy in situ nanocomposite, the hydroxyl and carboxylic acid stretching frequencies of CDs in FTIR at 3492 and 1730cm−1, respectively, are shifted to 3444 and 1764cm−1 after formation of the nanocomposite. In an 1H NMR study also the carboxylic acid and phenolic protons of pure CDs in the range of 7.5–9.5ppm are not present after the formation of the nanocomposite (as shown in Fig. 7.6 ), which further confirms that these functional groups participate in the reaction during the fabrication of the in situ nanocomposite. The homogeneous distribution of CDs due to the strong interfacial interactions with hyperbranched epoxy in the nanocomposite is also shown in TEM image, Fig. 7.7A. In another study, different wt% of CDs are incorporated during in situ polymerization of polyurethane (Gogoi et al., 2015). Here there are also various oxygenous functional groups of CDs that possess an inherent tendency to interact with polyurethane or polyurethane precursors either covalently or noncovalently. Such interactions play a central role toward the homogeneous distribution of the CDs over the polyurethane matrix, as shown in the TEM image (Fig 7.7B). Due to strong interactions between CDs and polymer matrices the graphitic spacing (002) of pure CDs is also enhanced, as evident from the HR-TEM images (Fig. 7.7C).

The formation of polymer-CD nanocomposites with uniform dispersion and strong interfacial interactions dramatically improves the physical, mechanical, and thermal properties, etc. and provides a new set of desired properties (e.g., fluorescence, chemical, optical, electrical, biological, and light harvesting).

The physical properties of the pristine polymers are effectively changed after the formation of nanocomposites with CDs because of the different physical and chemical interactions of the polar groups that are present in both polymer matrix and CDs. For example the numbers of epoxy (394geq−1) as well as hydroxyl groups (100mg KOH per g) of the hyperbranched epoxy were enhanced to 360geq−1 and 144mg KOH per g, respectively, after the formation of in situ nanocomposites with CDs (De et al., 2015). The viscosity of the hyperbranched epoxy is also enhanced to 1.5-folds, due to the presence of more intra- and intermolecular attractions compared to the pristine epoxy. Again, in the case of nanocomposite fabricated through solution technique with the same amount of CDs, the viscosity increases more than fourfold, as all the polar functional groups of CDs are totally free to interact with polar functional groups of the hyperbranched epoxy (De et al., 2015). These interactions also effectively change the rheological properties of the pristine polymers after the formation of the nanocomposite with CDs. It is similarly seen that the viscosity of a hyperbranched polyester is enhanced after the formation of nanocomposites with CDs due to interactions that moderately restrict the segmental movements of the polymer chains, and the viscosities of the nanocomposites are increased with the loadings of the CDs (Hazarika and Karak, 2016). However, the viscosities of the nanocomposites remain constant with the constant shear or with shear rate at constant temperature, similar to pristine polyester. It is also observed that the shear viscosity of the nanocomposites and the pristine system decreased with the increase of temperature due to the decrease in intermolecular interactions between the CDs and polyester segments as the kinetic energy of the nanocomposites increased. Whereas in the case of viscoelastic properties the loss modulus (G″) and storage modulus (G′) of the nanocomposites with the applied frequency revealed that both the values are increased with the increase of frequency at different rates as well as with the loadings of CD. The values of G′ compared to G″ over the entire frequency region are higher in magnitude due to the improvement of elastic behavior of the polyester upon incorporation of CD. A strong dependence of G′ on the interfacial energy is also found over G″. In the nanocomposites, the solid-like elastic behavior, G″< G′, dictated by the fusion of polymer-polymer and polymer-nanomaterial interactions signified the formation of a continuous network within the polyester matrix. The values are decreased with the increase of temperature, due to the increase of kinetic energy and free volume of the polymer chains with temperature, which decreases the entanglement density and intra- and intermolecular interactions within the polymer chains. Due to the same reasons, both the values of G′ and G″ increase relative to the loading of CD.

The presence of a large number of functional groups on the surface of CDs highly influences the chemical properties of the nanocomposites. The functional groups such as hydroxyl, epoxy, carboxylic acid or ester, and ether on the surface of CDs also take part in the curing reaction during the hardening of the nanocomposites of epoxy, polyester, or polyurethane (De et al., 2015; Hazarika and Karak, 2016). The crosslinked 3-D structures with different physical and chemical interactions of hyperbranched epoxies formed through solution and in situ techniques are shown in Fig. 7.5. This participation in the crosslinking reactions of CDs enhance the crosslink densities of the nanocomposites, which drastically improve the chemical and weather resistance of the pristine polymers (Hazarika and Karak, 2016). Owing to the different interactions of the polar functional groups of CDs along with the functional groups of polymers, with substrates, the adhesive strength of the pristine polymers (like epoxy, polyester, polyurethane, etc.) enhanced around 3–5-folds. Table 7.1 shows hyperbranched epoxy/CD nanocomposites using 0.1, 0.5, and 1.0wt% CDs, where curing time as well as swelling values of the nanocomposites decrease with the increase of CD loading. The adhesive strength of the nanocomposites was drastically enhanced to more than two- and threefold from the values of pristine epoxy using 1.0wt% CDs (De et al., 2013).

Mechanical properties of pristine polymers are significantly improved by the formation of nanocomposites with CDs. The uniform dispersion through strong interfacial interactions of the CDs due to the chemical bonding and different physical interactions of the oxygen functional groups on the surface of CDs with the polar functional groups of polymers drastically enhance the mechanical properties. Enhancement of mechanical properties like tensile strength, elongation at break, toughness, impact resistance, scratch hardness, and bending of hyperbranched epoxy/CD nanocomposites from the pristine epoxy are shown in Table 7.1. The stress-strain profiles of the nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 7.8A. More importantly the elongation at the break and the toughness (i.e., the area under the stress-strain curves) of the hyperbranched epoxy are dramatically improved by factors of 5 and 8, respectively, after the formation of nanocomposites with 1.0wt% of CD loading. The enhancement of the tensile strength of the polyester/CD nanocomposites using 0.1–1.0wt% of CDs is shown in stress-strain profiles in Fig. 7.8B, where the tensile strength has increased 2–6 times over the pristine polyester (Hazarika and Karak, 2016). This improved tensile strength is attributed to the homogeneous dispersion of CDs into the polyester matrix and the strong interfacial interactions, such as hydrogen bonding or possible ionic interactions with polyester matrix. The polar functional groups of CDs also take part in the chemical and physical crosslinking reactions with the polymer matrix and hardener. Therefore the Young's modulus values of the nanocomposites are also increased with the CD loading, as shown in Fig. 7.8C. The thermal stability of the polyester was significantly enhanced after the formation of the nanocomposite. The initial degradation temperature of the polyester shifted from 234°C to 265°C after the formation of the nanocomposite with 1wt% of CD content (Hazarika and Karak, 2016). The increased thermal stability of the nanocomposites can be ascribed to high crosslinking density and secondary interactions imparted by CDs with the polyester chains. Furthermore the presence of strong covalent and noncovalent interactions between the CDs and polyester improves the thermal stability of the nanocomposites. These strong interactions also reduce the segmental motion of the polymer chains, and therefore the glass transition temperature (T g) of the polyester increases gradually from 49°C to 56°C after the formation of nanocomposites. The formation of polyurethane/CD nanocomposites, tensile strength, toughness, and scratch hardness of the polyurethane increased more than 4.5-, 4.0-, and 2.0-fold after the formation of the nanocomposites (Gogoi et al., 2015). The thermal stability of the polyurethane is also enhanced to 28°C by the formation of the nanocomposites. In another study a significant improvement in the mechanical properties is observed in terms of tensile strength (1.7-fold) and toughness (1.5-fold) as well as in thermal stability (20°C) of the pristine hyperbranched polyurethane by the formation of nanocomposite with 5wt% of Ag/CD nanohybrids (Duarah et al., 2016).

Generally, carbon-based nanocomposite materials, including polymer nanocomposites of carbon nanomaterial, display remarkable electrical properties. Although the core of the CDs possesses a graphitic aromatic structure, it exhibits poor electrical conductivity due to defects or a poor crystalline structure as well as the presence of a large number of oxygen functional groups. Therefore in general the conductivity of the CDs depends on the content of the functional groups or the number of sp2 and sp3 carbons. The reduction of the functionality increases the conductivity of CDs. Recently, it has been found that the conductivity of the CDs is increased by using conducting polymers as the additives (Bhattacharjee et al., 2015). Bhattacharjee et al. (2015) reported poly(4-styrene sulfonate) (PSS) stabilized conducting CDs (PSS-CDs). The conducting behavior of the PSS-CDs was studied through a conductive atomic force microscope, where the spreading resistance images (SRI) coated on ITO show bright dots on a dark background in 2-D topology, as shown in Fig. 7.9A. The 3-D projection contributed to the sheer increase of current (i.e., low resistances) at those points. The low resistance points are possibly conductive PSS-CDs deposited on the ITO. The 3-D SRI image shows several sharp peaks standing over an area of 1μm×1μm, as shown in Fig. 7.9B. Again the I–V measurements (between −1.2 and +1.2V) of the PSS-CDs on various spots (in SRI 2-D image) shows a linear behavior at a low bias and a sudden jump in current value as the bias crossed zero voltage, as shown in Fig. 7.9C. Linearity in the low-bias range and the fact that current steeply reaches saturation reveal that PSS-CDs are natural conductors. This conductivity may be due to the CC sp2 hybridization that allows for the overlapping of π electrons of the adjacent carbon atoms and PSS stabilized surface sites. Recently, Pal et al. (2016) synthesized conducting polymer nanocomposites comprised of CDs and polypyrrole (PPy), which exhibited a higher electrical conductivity in comparison to the components CDs and PPy. The current vs voltage (I–V) characteristic plot (Fig. 7.10A) for CD film indicates the electrically conducting nature of CDs, where the linear I–V characteristic plot shows metallic like behavior of the film. The conductivity of the CD film could be attributed to the presence of sp2 CC bonds, which allow for the conjugation of the adjacent π-bonds to form the π- and π⁎-bands. Further incorporation of CD into the PPy matrix improves the conductivity of PPy (Fig. 7.10B). The maximum conductivity for the nanocomposite film was found to be 2.60mSm−1, while that for PPy film was 0.23mSm−1. PPy is a p-type of semiconductor, which mainly conducts through holes. Therefore the conductivity of the nanocomposite is enhanced as the positive charge density increases in the polymer backbone. The charge carrier density in the nanocomposite might have been enhanced in the presence of CDs, which are excellent electron acceptors.

The optical properties of CDs from their inception are the most frequently studied. PL is the most fascinating features and classic signature of CDs. Despite the diversity in structure, all types of CDs are generally prepared from various carbon precursors by different approaches and display a similar kind of PL behavior, which shows the highest intense emissive peak in the visible region (400–500nm) at an excitation wavelength of 340–380nm. Most of the reported CDs exhibit emission wavelength-dependent excitation behavior, and the studies demonstrated that this type of PL behavior of CDs is mainly controlled by the size of the graphitic core and the functional groups present on the surface (Li et al., 2012a,b; Zhu et al., 2015; De and Karak, 2017). They also exhibit up-conversion luminescence and photo-induced electron/charge transfer properties, which are most fascinating optical properties of CDs. As the photo-excited CDs can act both as excellent electron donors and electron acceptors the PL intensity of CDs could be quenched efficiently either by electron acceptors or electron donor molecules. Therefore, the polymer/CD nanocomposite-based solid films are the potential materials for advanced optoelectronic applications. With the incorporation of a very small amount of CDs into the polymer matrices the solid nanocomposite films shows strong and tunable PL property. The quantum size (below 10nm) and high dispersion ability of CDs also help to retain the transparency of the transparent polymers. Zhou et al. (2013) fabricated light-emitting amphibious CDs nanocomposite films with poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) in DCM and with poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) in water through simple blending followed by solvent evaporation. The incorporation of CDs provides strong PL properties to the polymer films without compromising their transparent properties (Fig 7.11 ). The CDs-PMMA and CDs-PVA films containing 1wt% of CDs are almost colorless and highly transparent as demonstrated by the UV-vis transmittance spectra (Fig. 7.11A). The optical properties of the nanocomposite films are quite stable so no obvious decrease in PL and absorption can be detected, even under UV light (365nm) for 24h. The incorporation of CDs into PEG to produce PEG/CD nanocomposite solid films also exhibited a strong and tunable blue-red emission, where a high fluorescent quantum yield of 12.6% was achieved (Hao et al., 2014). Epoxy-, polyester-, or polyurethane-based CD nanocomposites emitted green and blue fluorescence after illumination with short (254nm) and long (365nm) wavelengths of UV light (De et al., 2013, 2015; Gogoi et al., 2015; Hazarika and Karak, 2016). This is ascribed to the existence of different surface states and size distribution of CDs inside these polymer matrices. More interestingly, photo-excited CDs can act both as an excellent electron donor and an electron acceptor, therefore the polymer/CD nanocomposites exhibited interesting up-conversion luminescence, as well as photo-induced electron and charge transfer properties. It is revealed that a nanocomposite of a polyester/CD as well as the pure CD exhibited up-conversion emission spectra at 430–530nm upon exciting at wavelengths 650–800nm. This feature is attributed to the presence of different emissive states and size distributions of the CDs as well as the active multiphoton excitation processes (Hazarika and Karak, 2016). In the case of semiconductor metal/CD nanohybrid-based polymer nanocomposites the up-conversion PL of CDs is employed to transfer lower-energy photons into higher-energy ones, which can excite the conduction band electrons of the semiconductor metals according to their band gaps (De et al., 2014).

Most prominently, CDs are mostly nontoxic and environmentally friendly, as the structural backbone is biomolecules. These behaviors make CDs very desirable as alternatives to semiconductor quantum dots to visualize biological systems both in vitro and in vivo (Mehta et al., 2014). In general the carbon cores of CDs themselves are not toxic; in fact, any cytotoxicity of CDs is primarily due to the presence of oxygen functional groups on the surface of the CDs. Human hepatocellular carcinoma cells show a >90% survival rate after exposure to CDs at a concentration of 0.5mgmL−1, which is higher than the concentration necessary for bioimaging studies (Ray et al., 2009). Many other studies have also been proven to pose negligible cytotoxicity to many other cell lines (Zhao et al., 2008; Yang et al., 2009; Li et al., 2010a). Therefore, currently polymer/CD nanocomposites are the potent materials for different bioimaging, bone tissue engineering, and biosealant in surgical wound skin closure applications. The biocompatible polymers further reduces the toxicity of the nanocomposite materials. Recently, we have performed in vitro cell proliferation/cytocompatibility studies using human dermal fibroblast (HDF) and human keratinocyte (HaCaT) cells on hyperbranched epoxy/CD nanocomposites, fabricated through both solution and in situ techniques (De et al., 2015). The nanocomposites exhibited good biocompatibility with HDF and HaCaT cells with good viability, spreading, and proliferation of these cells on the films. An Alamar blue assay has been done to quantify cell viability and proliferation over time to assess cytocompatibility of the nanocomposites, in which increased reduction percentage of Alamar blue relates overtime to a higher number of proliferating cells which in turn indicates cytocompatibility of the materials favoring cell growth. The nanocomposite films showed cell proliferation and an increase in cell number with time. After 7days of culture, proliferation of both the cells displayed the following trend: ∼82% on in situ fabricated nanocomposite film, ∼44% on solution fabricated nanocomposite film, and ∼16% on pure epoxy film compared to day 1 of culture. Fig. 7.12 shows the fluorescent microscopic images of both HDF and HaCaT cells attached firmly to all the nanocomposites and proliferated well. The cells maintain native spindle morphology, which suggest that the nanocomposites are cytocompatible as well as favorable for growth and attachment. Cells are also evenly distributed on the nanocomposite films. In a later, we also found biocompatibility for in vitro adhesion, proliferation, and differentiation of MG 63 osteoblast cells with good cell viability on polyurethane/CD nanocomposite films (Gogoi et al., 2015).

The incorporation CDs in polymer matrices drastically improves the physical, mechanical, thermal, electrical, and biological properties with introducing a new set of desired properties like optical, photochemical, and electrochemical. Therefore the polymer nanocomposites of CDs are the attractive materials in the fields of optoelectronics, catalysis, biological, sensors, and energy applications.

Kwon et al. (2013) derived nitrogen-rich CDs from poly(acrylamide) (PAA) through emulsion-templated carbonization, which emits bright visible light under UV illumination. Therefore, they fabricated a large-area freestanding nanocomposite film of the CDs and poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) matrix-based nanocomposite. The flexible, nontoxic, and mechanically robust luminescent film exhibits a novel type of phosphor, which have intrinsically prevented luminescence quenching to gain a strong solid-state luminescence. A fabricated white LED rendering the emission spectrum by the combination of nanocomposite film with an InGaN blue (400nm) LED is shown in Fig. 7.13 . The cool white light with the CIE (Commission Internationale d’Éclairage) coordinates of (0.3664, 0.4484) and the correlated color temperature (CCT) of 5080.4K are observed by applying a forward-bias current of 50mA (∼3V, optimized). The light is almost identical to that emitted from conventional tubular fluorescent lamps, and the luminous efficacy of radiation is obtained 108.19lmWopt −1, which is comparable to nanocrystal or compound phosphors. Chen et al. (2015) also fabricated a white LED by coating amido group-functionalized CDs/epoxy nanocomposites on a high-performance blue InGaN chip (465-nm). The blue LED with out applying nanocomposite coating, emitted blue light centered at 465nm, which after coating of the nanocomposite, the intensity of the blue light weakens, accompanied by the presence of a broad band peaked at 552nm. However, the emission intensities increased relative to the current from 20 to 220mA at 465 and 552nm. It has been observed that white light is only obtained after encapsulation. Zhou et al. (2013) synthesized a multifunctional hydrogel through the crosslinking of poly(acrylamide) in the presence of poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) and CDs at room temperature for light-emitting polymer applications. The obtained hydrogel is highly transparent and shows a strong blue emission under UV light (365nm) at 20°C owing to the well dispersion of the CDs in the polymer matrix. With the increase of temperature from 20°C to 40°C, the hydrogel became opaque, and the PL intensity of the hydrogel under UV light decreased severely. The decrease of this PL intensity is found sharp at around 32°C due to the lower critical solution temperature (LCST). The fluorescent-functionalized CDs are also polymerized through a copolymerizing technique with several model monomers that produce different functional fluorescent polymeric materials in solution or in the solid-state (Zhang et al., 2012). The copolymer of CD and N-isopropylacrylamide emits a strong blue fluorescence in solution even at above LCST, >33°C. Again the fabricated 3-D copolymer of CD and methyl methacrylate at solid state exhibits transparent and highly fluorescent hybrid materials, which shows an optical-limiting effect. This optical-limiting effect is accompanied by a CD concentration-dependent PL red-shift, which arises from the self-absorbing/excitation effect.

The interesting solid-phase fluorescent properties of CD/polymer nanocomposites are also highly exciting materials for printing inks of multicolor patterning or anticounterfeit applications (De et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2013a). Functional CD/polymer nanocomposites are capable of producing multicolor patterns on the microscale that could potentially be used in anticounterfeit applications. Different delicate designs, like logos and fingerprints made by using the nanocomposites ink in liquid or solution state and followed by drying, shows colorless under visible light but resulted in a fluorescent color under exposure UV lights. An example of hyperbranched epoxy/CD nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 7.14A. UV light-switchable transparent nanocomposite polymer films and invisible luminescent inks were also prepared by using PVA and a trisdipicolinate lanthanide-CD complex for advanced optical devices and anticounterfeiting technologies (Wang et al., 2016). The emission colors of the resulting nanocomposite films and inks can be switched from the multiple colors of lanthanide complexes to the blue color of CDs depending on the change in UV light illumination from 254 to 365nm with different mass ratios, as shown in Fig. 7.14B. The highly transparent films exhibited bright various emission colors, including green, yellow, orange, and red under 254nm UV light illumination at different mass ratios (samples 1–4 in Fig. 7.14B). However, the emission colors of all these films changed to a deep blue color upon switching the wavelength of UV light from 254 to 365nm. Therefore the luminescence patterns printed by these inks can offer two luminescent security elements in one tag or label for better authenticating items in anticounterfeit applications. Further, Zhu et al. (2013a,b) fabricated nanocomposites by processing of CDs with different polymers; for example, PVA/CD nanofibers by electrospinning and bulk poly N,N′-dimethylacrylamide (PDMAA)/CD nanocomposites by polymerizing CDs in a DMAA solution. The PVA/CD nanocomposites exhibited multicolor patterns of fiber under UV, blue, and green light excitation. Again the PL intensity of bulk PDMAA/CD nanocomposites was unchanged after 2000W UV exposure for 30min. The printed patterns and prepared nanocomposites also retained their stability after 4months in an indoor environment, which is useful for practical applications.

The photo-induced electron and charge transfer ability as well as the up-conversion PL properties of CDs and CD-based nanohybrids are gaining great attention in different catalytic applications. However, the practical difficulty associated with CDs or its nanohybrid systems in effective photocatalysis is owing to their solubility or high dispersibility in aqueous/organic phase, which gives rise to its tedious isolation from the reaction medium. Therefore the polymeric matrices provide the best way to promote the catalytic activity of CDs or CD-based materials on account of prohibiting solid-state quenching and permitting the straightforward isolation of the catalyst. The photo-induced CDs are excellent electron donors as well as acceptors, which result in the efficient separation of electrons and holes. These electron/hole pairs react with H2O and O2 to produce active oxygen radicals like •OH and •O– 2, which take part in the catalytic reactions. Again, an optical phenomenon the up-conversion PL of CDs is employed to transfer photons with lower energy into ones with higher energy, which can considerably extend the sunlight absorption of wide bandgap semiconductors into the visible region and even the near-infrared region. Therefore polymer/CD nanocomposites serve as the versatile components in photocatalyst design. Mosconi et al. (2015) fabricated different N-doped CDs/polymer nanocomposites using polyamide, polyurea-urethane, polyester, poly(methyl methacrylate), etc. polymers to explore them in several photochemical reactions under UV and visible lights like photoreduction of silver ions to silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), selective photo-oxidation of benzylalcohol to the benzaldehyde, photocatalytic generation of H2, etc. EPR spectroscopy is applied to check the capability of N-doped CDs to generate paramagnetic species such as radicals or long-lived excited triplet states upon light absorption. All the N-doped CD/polymer nanocomposites show photoinduced formation of radicals under visible light absorption, as demonstrated by the appearance of weak lines in their EPR spectra in Fig. 7.15A. The radical formation after light absorption is due to photoinduced electron transfer processes from the N-doped CD to the surrounding molecules to produce radicals in the polymeric nanocomposites. However, the radicals are unstable and easily undergo fast reactions, which scavenge the radicals and prevent their EPR detection. Photoinduced electron transfer is probably not an efficient process in the nanocomposites due to the lack of a suitable electron acceptor. Therefore, photochemical activity of N-doped CDs in the polymeric matrices represents a promising result of the application of the nanocomposites in photoactive materials when a good electron acceptor is added. The time-resolved EPR technique (TREPR) with microsecond time resolution is used to observe well-defined EPR spectra shortly after a visible laser pulse on the nanocomposites, as shown in Fig. 7.15B. The absence of TREPR spectra in the nanocomposites is attributed to efficient quenching of the excited states promoted by intermolecular interactions. The spectral simulation in Fig. 7.15C defines the magnetic dipolar interaction between the two unpaired electrons of the triplet state. Therefore the results suggests that the EPR signal can be assigned to the molecular excited triplet state of the N-doped CDs. Recently a waterborne polyurethane/CD nanocomposite system is employed as an efficient, resourceful, and sustainable photocatalyst for the paraselective hydroxylation of substituted aromatic compounds using H2O2 under UV light (Das et al., 2017). Both the polymer matrix and CD generate a synergistic catalytic system, where the unique structural feature of the functionalities in this nanocomposite system attracts the aromatic substrates into close proximity and activates them. Additionally the flexible molecular architecture of the hyperbranched polyurethane provides favorable interactions with the substrates having various sizes, which increases accessibility of the active sites.

Previously, we performed a photocatalytic degradation of an anthropogenic chemical, namely organophosphate pesticide (ethyl paraoxon), under solar light using a heterogeneous catalyst of hyperbranched epoxy-supported CD-reduced Cu2O nanohybrid (De et al., 2014). In this catalytic degradation reaction the nanohybrid-based epoxy composite degrades the pesticide within 5h. However, the pure CD based nanocomposite only degrades 50% of the pesticide, even at 12h. In this photocatalytic reaction the mechanism of degradation is the same as described above by up-conversion luminescence and efficient separation of electrons and holes. The functional groups of the polymer matrix also played a supporting role in interacting with the polar pesticide molecules. Furthermore the recovery of the used catalyst from the reaction medium is very easy, and the nanocomposite showed same catalytic performance even after 5 times reused. Again, the same organophosphate pesticide has been degraded within 2 h, using a polymer nanocomposite catalyst of RGO-Ag-RCD and polyurethane (Duarah and Karak, 2018). It also degrades aniline and rhodamine B upon exposure of solar light for 4h and 1h 10min, respectively. Here the AgNPs are utilized to capture solar light, with a reduction in the effective band gap of RGO and AgNPs. The RGO-Ag-RCD nanohybrid delayed the recombination of the e−/h+ pair of RGO, which allows an effective charge separation and photocatalytic activity. Further the electrostatic interaction between cationic AgNPs and anionic RGO and RCD leads to a strong interaction among them, which enhances the above factors. The electrostatic force among RCD, RGO, and AgNPs provides tunneling of photo-excited electrons, which are utilized for photodegradation. In addition the presence of polar peripheral functional groups and the high surface area of the nanocomposite make it accessible to the organic compounds.

Fluorescent CDs are very attractive in biological applications, as they do not show any visible signs of toxicity in animals and thus can be used for in vivo studies. Therefore, biocompatible CD/polymer nanocomposites are highly potent materials for bioimaging and bone/tissue engineering applications. Very recently, Demir et al. (2018) coupled CDs with molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) for the targeting and imaging of cancer cells, as shown in Fig. 7.16 . The nanocomposite system is employed as a biocompatible optical imaging tool for probing cancer biomarkers. Here the fluorescent emission of CDs is used as an internal light source for the photopolymerization of a thin MIP shell around them to fabricate the nanocomposite. The nanocomposite offers safer alternatives, coupled with selective biotargeting capabilities compared to other materials due to the recognition cavities present on the MIP, which renders these nanocomposites powerful tools for the imaging of tumor biomarkers. The quantified confocal fluorescence micrographs to detect hyaluronic acid on keratinocytes and HeLa cells (Fig. 7.16), indicates that there are ∼4 times more CD-MIP glucuronic acid (GlcA) on HeLa cells than bound to HaCaT cells. This confirmed that the CD-MIPGlcA is capable of differentiating between cells with different hyaluronan content, such as healthy cells and tumors.

Again a tannic acid-based, water-dispersible hyperbranched polyurethane nanocomposite has been fabricated with a bionanohybrid of CDs and peptides to impart a target-specific in vivo bone healing ability for bone tissue engineering (Gogoi et al., 2017). An in vitro assessment of the nanocomposite reveals good osteoblast adhesion, proliferation, and differentiation, as well as exhibits in vivo ectopic bone formation ability assisted by the panel of peptides. The in vivo mineralization and vascularization studied also reveal the occurrence of calcification and blood vessel formation. Gao et al. (2016) has designed nanocomposite microcapsules for tracking and drug delivery vehicles through in-situ hydrothermal method using fluorescent CDs and polyelectrolyte, as shown in Fig. 7.17 . The freestanding microcapsules show a high biocompatibility with excellent luminescence that facilitates imaging and identification in vitro, which is able to load small molecules and ultrasound responsiveness to trigger their release. The in situ-formed highly fluorescent CDs in capsule shells also allows these capsules an ultrasound responsiveness and reduced permeability.

Polymer/CD nanocomposites are also utilized in smart bioengineering applications like shape-memory materials (Duarah et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2016), which are able to deform and fix into a temporary shape under specifically external conditions and subsequently recover their original shape upon exposure to an appropriate stimulus. Yang et al. (2016) reported a PVA/CD nanocomposite, which possessed tunable water-induced shape-memory behavior in different pH and temperature environments. Here the oxygen and active hydrogen-rich CDs serve as extra physical crosslinking points in PVA via a strong hydrogen-bonding interaction, which largely improves the shape memory performances of PVA. Water successfully actuates the shape recovery of the deformed shape of the nanocomposite at room temperature. The water-induced shape recovery is mainly attributed to the plasticizing effect of water and its competitive hydrogen bonding. Again the shape recovery time of this nanocomposite is tunable by changing the pH value and temperature, as shown in Fig. 7.18 . CDs also provides an excellent luminescent property to the nanocomposite, which makes the shape change visible under UV light (Fig. 7.18). Duarah et al. (2016) also reported a hyperbranched polyurethane/CD-Ag nanocomposite-based rapid self-expandable stent, which exhibited notable shape recovery (99.6%) and nearly complete self-expansion (>99%) just within 20s at (37±1)°C.

Polymer/CD nanocomposites have also been gaining tremendous attention in sensors due to the interesting photo-induced electron transfer, as well as the excellent static and dynamic fluorescence quenching of CDs. An in-situ fabricated conducting polymer nanocomposite of CDs and polypyrrole (PPy) is used for the highly sensitive and selective detection of picric acid (PA) in water as well as in soil based on the changes in I–V characteristics (Pal et al., 2016). The distinct concentration-dependent changes in the I–V characteristics of the nanocomposite in the presence of PA indicates a higher degree of protonation to the aromatic ring in the PPy backbone, which allows for a higher charge carrier density. Fig. 7.19A shows that the response of the nanocomposite film is much higher toward PA in comparison to other nitrophenol derivatives, which indicates a selectivity for the preferential detection of PA. The limit of detection is observed to be 1.40×10−7 M (32ppb). The nanocomposite is also able to detect a trace amount of PA present in the soil sample with appreciable changes in I–V characteristics at low concentrations, where the minimum concentration of PA is detected 5.7ngmg−1 of the soil. A selective and ecofriendly sensor is designed through a microwave-assisted grafting of MIP onto the surface of CDs for the detection of tetracycline (Tc) (Hou et al., 2016), as shown in Fig. 7.19B. The figure also shows that the relative fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposites decreases linearly with the increase of concentration of tetracycline from 20nM to 14μM under optimal conditions. The detection limit of tetracycline is found to be 5.48nM. MIP-coated CDs nanocomposites are also used to detect trace tetracycline in fish via synchronous fluorescence quenching (Yang et al., 2018) and bisphenol A via fluorescent approaches (Liu et al., 2016). The MIP-CDs nanocomposite exhibits strong fluorescent property and high selectivity as it incorporated both merits of CDs and MIP. In these strategies, fluorescent CDs act as the signal component while MIP film serves as the recognition element. Again an in situ-fabricated polyaniline nanofiber (PN)/CD nanohybrid is used as an efficient fluorimetric sensor for the detection of As(III) with a limit up to 0.001ppb (Saikia and Karak, 2018). The combination of CD and PN acts as an effective donor-acceptor pair, which results into efficient energy transfer system in PN/CD. The delocalization of π electrons of PN is also a key factor for the effective detection with this lower limit.

CDs have excellent photo-absorption capacity with electron-accepting and transport properties, which provide new and interesting opportunities to use CDs or CD-based materials in energy conversion and storage applications like solar cells/photovoltaic and supercapacitor/battery (Li et al., 2015; De and Karak, 2017; De et al., 2017a,b). Recently, polymer/CD nanocomposites have been widely used in solar cell and energy storage applications owing to their high conductivity, excellent photo-absorption capacity as well as electron accepting and transport properties. Choi et al. (2013) fabricated a solution-processable polymer solar cell (PSC) using the surface plasmon resonance effect of CD-supported silver nanoparticles (CD-Ag), which are utilized as plasmonic materials in PSC. The surface plasmon resonance of the nanohybrid allows for significant radiative emission and additional light absorption that lead to remarkable enhancements of the internal quantum efficiency in PSC. The PSC shows power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 8.31% and an internal quantum efficiency of 99%, compared with a control device (PCE of 7.53% and internal quantum efficiency of 91%). Fig. 7.20A shows the used PSC device structure of a glass/ITO/CD-Ag/PEDOT:PSS/PTB7:PC71BM/Al. Fig. 7.20B and C show the current density versus voltage (J–V) characteristics and external quantum efficiency (EQE) of the devices with and without CD-Ag NPs, where the device without CD-Ag NPs shows J sc: 14.4mAcm−2, V oc: 0.75V, FF: 0.70 and PCE: 7.53%, whereas the device with CD-Ag NPs shows a J sc of 16.0mAcm−2, V oc of 0.75V, FF of 0.70 and PCE of 8.31%. Therefore an improvement in the PCE is achieved, which is due to the increase in J sc caused by the enhancement of EQE in the range 400–700nm, as shown in Fig. 7.20C, whereas V oc and FF remained the same. The enhancement in absorption (Δa) and EQE (ΔEQE) of the CD-Ag NPs clearly show the effect of CD-Ag NPs on the photogeneration of charge carriers (Fig. 7.20D). Thus the results demonstrate that the nanocomposite system constitutes a versatile and effective route to achieve a high-performance polymer solar cell. Very recently, Zhang et al. (2018) also designed a high-efficiency inverted PSC through the solution-processable bilayer ZnO/CDs as the electron extraction layer, which helps to suppress the exciton quenching by passivating the ZnO surface defects. This leads to an enhanced exciton dissociation, a reduced charge recombination, and more efficient charge extraction probability, thereby achieving 27% higher PCE (∼9.64%) compared to a control cell (∼7.59%). Again a luminescent CD-filled polysiloxane nanocomposite is fabricated through the hydrolytic condensation between KH791 and CDs to enhance PCE of a polymer fullerene-based bulk heterojunction solar cell by utilizing the basis of the luminescent downshifting behavior of the nanocomposite (Huang et al., 2014). The PCE is increased by about 12% due to an effective light conversion of near-ultraviolet and blue-violet portions of sunlight.

Attributes of unique and tunable physicochemical, optical, electronic, toxicity, and biosafety properties of CDs encourage extensive research of the synthesis, modifications, properties, and applications of CDs since their discovery in 2004. However the research of polymer/CD nanocomposites is just intensified from this current decade. The presence of large number of oxygen polar functional groups and graphitic structure with quantum dot size of CDs and CD-based nanohybrids fascinates to integrate them easily into the different polymer matrices with uniform dispersion and strong interfacial interactions. Therefore, by the formation of polymer nanocomposites with a very small amount of CDs significantly improves physical, mechanical, thermal, electrical and biological properties, as well as introducing a new set of attractive properties to enhance the application area of the virgin polymer. The interesting optical properties, including upconversion PL, photo-induced electron/hole, or charge transfer properties of CDs, produce a highly efficient and tunable nanocomposite for photocatalysis, energy storage, or harvesting and sensor applications with different polymer matrices. Again the polymer/CD nanocomposites are the attractive materials for biomedical applications, like bioimaging and targeting, drug delivery, bone/tissue engineering, and so on owing to the introduction of biosafety, biocompatibility, and toxicity aspects, along with the active functionality and interesting fluorescence properties.

Although significant progress has been made in the area of CDs and its different nanocomposites over the past decade, there are still many issues worthy of further exploration. Research progress on polymer/CD nanocomposite is also in its early stages as compared to graphene or other carbon-based polymer nanocomposites. The PL spectra of CDs and CD-based nanocomposites are commonly too broad due to the large heterogeneity of the synthesized products. More efforts should be devoted to develop a better understanding of the tunable PL properties, controllable and scalable synthesis, defects, surface engineering, and well-defined/atom-precise structure of CDs and CD-based nanocomposites. The more studies and clarification needs to realize the mechanisms of PL, up-conversion PL, photo-induced electron/charge transfer, etc. The electrical conductivity and crystallinity of CDs and polymer/CD nanocomposites remain as shortcomings for advanced electronic applications compared to other carbon-based nanomaterials. Therefore, numerous stimulating challenges and opportunities are still waiting to explore the enormous prospective of CDs and polymer/CD nanocomposites in diverse field applications in the near future. It is hoped that this chapter might afford enriched information on the versatile functions and recent progress in CDs to go further in the development and practical applications of CD-based polymeric nanocomposites.
